_meta.notes: Five-beat arc for forsaken-revenant in the Forsaken Village. The Revenant returned to refuse, not to belong. Tonight, a name they once said no to is about to ask them again — first as a chapel bell at the third hour, then as a person walking up the road. The arc is to hold the watch, hold the refusal, hold the cost, until dawn. Win: vigil>=10 AND refusal>=10 at turn>=8. Lose: refusal<=0 (drawn back into the world they swore off).

beats.narrative: It is the third hour after midnight, and the road is yours alone. You did not promise to watch it; you watch it anyway. The wind off the field is cold and you are colder. The thing you are watching for has not come tonight. It will come on a night. You will be here for that one.

suggestedVerbs.label: watch the road
suggestedVerbs.description: let the empty road write itself across your awareness; nothing arrives that you have not accounted for

suggestedVerbs.label: endure the cold
suggestedVerbs.description: the hour passes; you are still here, and still refusing

suggestedVerbs.label: refuse the warmth
suggestedVerbs.description: the village's residual heat asks you to come in; you do not

beats.narrative: The chapel bell finds itself rung. No hand pulls it; the wind is not enough to move that bell. The note that comes out of it is a name. It is a name you said no to once. You said no to it twice. You have not been asked a third time in a season.

suggestedVerbs.label: refuse the bell
suggestedVerbs.description: say no to the name without ceremony; let the bell finish unanswered

suggestedVerbs.label: watch the bell finish
suggestedVerbs.description: let it ring itself out; do not move toward it, do not move away

suggestedVerbs.label: leave the chapel's reach
suggestedVerbs.description: walk to the village edge before the third toll lands

fires.detail: chapel bell tolling a name at the third hour

beats.narrative: The bell did not ring itself. A figure walks up the western road — slow, deliberate, not afraid of you. You know the gait. You know the gait better than you know your own name now, on most nights. They stop at a respectful distance. They have not yet spoken. They are about to.

suggestedVerbs.label: watch them speak
suggestedVerbs.description: let them finish the asking before you answer; refusing without hearing is not refusing

suggestedVerbs.label: refuse before they speak
suggestedVerbs.description: your no arrives first; the asking will land on it

suggestedVerbs.label: hold the watch through their plea
suggestedVerbs.description: they will speak; the watch will hold; the refusal is the refusal of the watch, not of the words

beats.narrative: The hour is past four now. The watch grows heavy in a way that is not about sleep. The petitioner has said what they came to say, or they have not, but the asking is in the air either way and the air is colder than it was. Refusing the second time is harder than refusing the first.

suggestedVerbs.label: hold the cost
suggestedVerbs.description: the watch takes from your vigil tonight; you accept the subtraction

suggestedVerbs.label: refuse a second time
suggestedVerbs.description: your no, said again, costs more than the first; spend the cost cleanly

suggestedVerbs.label: return to the road's edge
suggestedVerbs.description: back to the vantage where the watch is yours; the petitioner can stand or go

beats.narrative: The east edge of the field turns the colour of a refusal that has held all night. The petitioner is gone — not driven off, not won over; they understood, and walked back the way they came. The road is yours again, and the watch was kept. Tomorrow's third hour will find you here. Tonight's already has.

suggestedVerbs.label: watch the dawn
suggestedVerbs.description: the road accepts your watch as the night accepted it; nothing else needs to happen

suggestedVerbs.label: one last hold
suggestedVerbs.description: the hour completes itself; you completed yourself with it

suggestedVerbs.label: refuse the morning's offer
suggestedVerbs.description: the day asks you to come inside; your no is older than the day's asking
